refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the ability to measure and report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a target value. The latter is a performance constraint or acceptance criterion, not a functional requirement of the system itself. The system's functional role is to provide visibility into energy consumption and work output so that stakeholders can assess performance against targets. Achievement of the target is a validation outcome dependent on operational practices, equipment tuning, and worksite conditions—not solely a system design obligation. This refinement keeps the FR in the problem domain (what must be measured and communicated) and removes the solution-dependent assumption that the system design alone 'achieves' the target.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that the NGHE solution captures and reports energy_used (kWh or equivalent) and ton_moved (or equivalent work unit) over defined evaluation periods, with sufficient granularity and accuracy to support comparison against E_target. Confirm that data is accessible to Site Manager and System Engineer for analysis.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall measure and record total energy consumed by the system during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall measure and record the quantity of work output (e.g., mass moved, volume displaced) during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall compute and report the energy efficiency metric (energy per unit work output) over agreed evaluation periods.
FR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall provide energy efficiency data to authorized stakeholders (Site Manager, System Engineer) in a format suitable for performance assessment and decision-making.
refined_fr.note: The refined FR is atomic in intent but is best decomposed into the four sub-FRs above, each addressing a distinct measurement, computation, or reporting obligation. Each sub-FR is solution-neutral and measurable.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument the energy source (fuel/power input) with continuous measurement and logging; instrument the work output (tonnage moved) with independent measurement and logging; compute energy_per_ton as a derived metric post-collection.
DP2: Embed real-time energy and work sensors into the primary equipment; stream both signals to a unified data acquisition system; calculate energy_per_ton continuously with periodic reporting.

DP3: Establish energy accounting via fuel consumption records (manual or automated pump logs) and work accounting via load cells or weighing stations; reconcile both streams offline to compute energy_per_ton at reporting intervals.

DP4: Deploy a hybrid approach: continuous real-time measurement of energy via instrumentation and work via sensors, with fallback to manual reconciliation logs for validation; compute energy_per_ton via both paths and flag discrepancies.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP2: Embed real-time energy and work sensors into the primary equipment; stream both signals to a unified data acquisition system; calculate energy_per_ton continuously with periodic reporting.

potential_sources: Energy measurement and work measurement are logically independent (different physical phenomena), but both feed a single derived metric calculation—this is acceptable coupling at the DP level because the calculation is a pure function.
Data acquisition system must handle two distinct sensor streams; risk of timing skew if sensors sample asynchronously—mitigated by timestamping and buffering strategy (deferred to IM/DP detail).

Unified reporting system depends on both streams being available; single point of failure if data pipeline breaks—mitigated by fallback logging (DP4 hybrid approach recommended for high-assurance contexts).
coupling_check.notes: DP2 avoids tight coupling of measurement logic to equipment design by treating sensors as independent inputs to a data aggregation layer. The derived metric (energy_per_ton) is decoupled from the measurement mechanism, allowing sensor technology substitution without logic redesign.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Energy source (fuel/power) is measurable at a single point (e.g., fuel pump, power meter). Risk: Distributed or multi-source energy systems may require multiple measurement points; FR may need refinement.

Assumption: Work output (tonnage) is measurable at a single point or aggregatable from multiple points. Risk: If work is distributed across multiple load points, measurement strategy must account for spatial distribution.

Assumption: Measurement accuracy and sensor drift are acceptable within the margin of E_target. Risk: Sensor calibration and maintenance procedures must be defined; acceptance criteria may need tolerance bands.

Assumption: Data collection and reporting infrastructure is available and reliable. Risk: Network outages, data loss, or system downtime could invalidate evaluation period. Recommend redundancy or offline fallback (DP4).

Assumption: Operational policies permit continuous instrumentation without safety or operational interference. Risk: Sensor installation, wiring, and data streaming may conflict with worksite safety plan; early coordination required.

Assumption: Energy_per_ton is the sole or primary metric for acceptance. Risk: If other efficiency metrics (e.g., energy per hour, energy per distance) are also required, DP2 must support multi-metric derivation without redesign.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) whether energy_per_ton is computed per shift, per day, or continuously; (2) whether multiple work output points (e.g., multiple load cells) must be aggregated or if a single measurement point is sufficient; (3) acceptable measurement uncertainty and sensor drift tolerance relative to E_target; (4) fallback or validation strategy if real-time data is unavailable. Consider adopting DP4 (hybrid real-time + offline reconciliation) if high assurance or regulatory compliance is required, as it decouples real-time reporting from measurement reliability and provides independent validation.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Unified data acquisition, sensor integration, energy calculation, and telemetry reporting system that embeds real-time energy and work sensors into primary equipment and streams both signals to a centralized processing pipeline.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Streams energy_per_ton metrics and periodic energy/work reports to external monitoring and analytics systems.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Provides physical/logical connectivity for sensor data ingestion from Vehicle equipment and outbound telemetry transmission.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operational context (e.g., load tonnage, mission parameters) to calibrate energy calculation and reporting intervals.

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest cohesion: sensor embedding, data acquisition, energy calculation, and reporting are all core to NGHE's energy-efficiency mission. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry output and tasking input cross boundaries; sensor hardware integration with Vehicle is a well-defined interface.

Poor fit: Vehicle is a physical platform, not a data processing system. Sensor embedding would be localized to Vehicle, but unified data acquisition, energy calculation, and reporting logic would diffuse into NGHE Solution. Creates tight coupling and split ownership.

Very poor fit: Teleoperation is a control/command interface, not a data acquisition or analytics system. Energy calculation and reporting have no functional relationship to remote operation. High diffusion of DP logic outside this module.

Misaligned: Safety Services handles alerts and safety constraints, not energy metering or efficiency analytics. Energy_per_ton calculation is orthogonal to safety. Significant diffusion and weak cohesion.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of DP2 because energy efficiency measurement and reporting is its core mission. The module boundary encompasses sensor integration strategy, unified data acquisition pipeline, energy calculation algorithms, and telemetry reporting—all tightly coupled to the NGHE value proposition. Vehicle provides the physical platform and sensor hardware; NGHE Solution consumes those signals and produces energy insights.
containment_risks: Sensor embedding into Vehicle hardware requires clear interface contract (e.g., signal format, sampling rate, calibration) to avoid tight coupling and rework if Vehicle design changes.
Network Link reliability and latency must be guaranteed; if streaming is interrupted, buffering and retry logic must be defined to prevent data loss.
Tasking interface must provide sufficient operational context (load tonnage, mission type) for accurate energy_per_ton normalization; incomplete tasking data will degrade metric validity.
Periodic reporting cadence and aggregation window must be specified to avoid ambiguity in energy calculation (e.g., per-cycle vs. per-hour vs. per-mission).

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP2 directly addresses the FR by specifying a complete solution path: embed sensors (energy + work), stream to unified acquisition, calculate energy_per_ton continuously, and report periodically. The DP is concrete, measurable, and traces to the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target). No logical gaps detected between requirement and design parameter.

diffusion_risks: Sensor embedding interface contract underspecified: signal format, sampling rate, calibration protocol, and hardware integration responsibility between Vehicle and NGHE Solution must be formalized to prevent rework and coupling drift.

Network Link reliability not addressed in DP: streaming interruption handling (buffering, retry, failover) is implicit in DP but not explicitly allocated to an IM. Risk of data loss if network latency or dropout occurs during critical measurement windows.

Tasking input dependency: DP relies on operational context (load tonnage, mission parameters) from external Tasking interface, but completeness and timeliness of this data are not contractually defined. Incomplete tasking data will invalidate energy_per_ton normalization.

Reporting cadence ambiguity: DP states 'periodic reporting' but does not specify aggregation window (per-cycle, per-hour, per-mission, per-shift). Different windows yield different energy_per_ton values; this must be locked to acceptance criteria evaluation period.

Energy calculation algorithm ownership: while NGHE Solution is the natural owner, the algorithm itself (formula, normalization factors, outlier handling) should be explicitly documented as a contained sub-module to prevent diffusion into Vehicle or external analytics systems.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves 0.92 containment score and is the correct ownership candidate. Sensor hardware integration with Vehicle is a well-defined external interface (inbound signal consumption). Telemetry output and Tasking input are clean boundary crossings. Primary risk is not diffusion of DP logic, but rather underspecification of interface contracts and internal algorithm documentation that could lead to implementation ambiguity and rework. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are correctly excluded from DP2 ownership (containment scores 0.45, 0.3, 0.25 respectively confirm poor fit).

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Acceptance criterion is measurable: energy_per_ton = energy_used / ton_moved ≤ E_target over agreed evaluation period. NGHE Solution IM boundary provides direct access to both energy and work signals, enabling calculation and reporting. Verification is feasible provided: (1) sensor accuracy and calibration are certified, (2) tasking data completeness is validated, (3) reporting cadence aligns with evaluation period definition, and (4) network link reliability is guaranteed during measurement windows.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR allocation to NGHE Solution IM is clear and unambiguous. However, allocation clarity depends on resolving four sub-allocations: (a) sensor embedding responsibility and interface contract (Vehicle → NGHE), (b) network link reliability SLA (Network Link → NGHE), (c) tasking data completeness and timeliness (Tasking → NGHE), and (d) reporting cadence and aggregation window (NGHE internal specification). These must be documented as part of IM boundary formalization to prevent allocation drift during implementation.

recommended_revisions: Formalize sensor embedding interface contract: specify signal format (voltage/current ranges, sampling rate, resolution), calibration procedure, hardware integration points, and responsibility split between Vehicle and NGHE Solution. Document as external interface specification.

Define network link reliability requirements: specify maximum acceptable latency, packet loss tolerance, buffering strategy for streaming interruptions, and failover/retry logic. Allocate to Network Link IM with SLA acceptance criteria.

Specify tasking input data contract: define required operational context fields (load tonnage, mission type, start/end timestamps), data freshness requirements, validation rules, and fallback behavior if tasking data is incomplete or delayed. Allocate to Tasking interface specification.

Lock reporting cadence and aggregation window: explicitly define periodic reporting interval (e.g., per-cycle, per-hour, per-mission) and aggregation window for energy_per_ton calculation. Ensure alignment with acceptance criteria evaluation period (e.g., if evaluation is per-shift, reporting must aggregate to shift-level granularity).

Document energy calculation algorithm as contained sub-module: specify formula (energy_used / ton_moved), normalization factors, outlier handling, rounding rules, and any calibration adjustments. Prevent diffusion by centralizing algorithm logic in a single NGHE Solution code/design artifact.

Add acceptance criteria traceability: map each containment risk to a specific acceptance criterion or test case (e.g., 'sensor calibration accuracy ±2%' → energy_per_ton measurement uncertainty budget; 'network uptime ≥99.5%' → data completeness for evaluation period).

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence in the logical mapping and ownership allocation. However, the DP-IM gate identifies six critical diffusion risks and underspecified interface contracts that cannot be resolved at the current level of abstraction. These risks are not design flaws but rather specification gaps that must be decomposed into explicit sub-FRs to formalize interface contracts, reliability requirements, and algorithm ownership. Given high safety criticality, proceeding to implementation without resolving these sub-allocations creates unacceptable rework and coupling drift risk. Decomposition is necessary to lock down interface specifications, SLAs, and internal algorithm documentation before detailed design.
next_level_sub_frs: FR2.1: Establish and maintain sensor embedding interface contract specifying signal format, sampling rate, resolution, calibration procedure, and hardware integration responsibility between Vehicle and NGHE Solution.

FR2.2: Guarantee network link reliability for energy and work signal streaming with defined latency, packet loss tolerance, buffering strategy, and failover/retry logic to prevent data loss during measurement windows.

FR2.3: Validate completeness and timeliness of tasking input data (load tonnage, mission parameters, timestamps) required for energy_per_ton normalization, with fallback behavior for incomplete or delayed data.

FR2.4: Define and enforce reporting cadence and aggregation window for energy_per_ton calculation, aligned with acceptance criteria evaluation period (e.g., per-shift, per-mission) to ensure consistent metric interpretation.

FR2.5: Encapsulate energy calculation algorithm (formula, normalization factors, outlier handling, calibration adjustments) as a contained sub-module within NGHE Solution to prevent diffusion and ensure single source of truth.

FR2.6: Establish sensor accuracy and calibration certification process with measurement uncertainty budget allocation to energy_per_ton acceptance criterion (e.g., ±2% calibration accuracy → energy_per_ton measurement tolerance).

revise.why: DP2 is logically sound but operationally incomplete. Revise DP2 to explicitly reference the six sub-DP allocations that will emerge from sub-FR decomposition: (1) sensor interface specification, (2) network reliability SLA, (3) tasking data validation contract, (4) reporting cadence lock, (5) algorithm encapsulation, (6) calibration certification. This creates a complete DP that traces all the way to implementation module boundaries and external interface contracts. Current DP2 is correct in concept but must be expanded to show how each diffusion risk is mitigated by a corresponding design decision.

assumptions: Sensor embedding interface can be formalized as a clean external contract between Vehicle and NGHE Solution without requiring Vehicle redesign.
Network link reliability can be guaranteed by existing infrastructure or by allocating buffering/retry logic to NGHE Solution IM without requiring new network hardware.

Tasking input data is available from an external Tasking system with sufficient completeness and timeliness to support energy_per_ton normalization; fallback behavior (e.g., default load assumptions) is acceptable if data is incomplete.
Reporting cadence can be locked to a single aggregation window (e.g., per-shift) that aligns with acceptance criteria evaluation period without requiring multiple reporting streams.

Energy calculation algorithm can be centralized in a single NGHE Solution code artifact (e.g., a dedicated module or class) without requiring distribution across Vehicle or external analytics systems.
Sensor calibration accuracy of ±2% is achievable and sufficient to meet energy_per_ton measurement uncertainty budget; calibration procedure can be formalized and certified before deployment.
open_risks: Sensor embedding interface contract may reveal incompatibilities between Vehicle hardware and NGHE Solution signal requirements, requiring Vehicle redesign or sensor replacement (high rework cost).
Network link reliability SLA may be unachievable with existing infrastructure, requiring new network hardware or fallback to local buffering with data loss risk.
Tasking input data completeness may be lower than assumed, requiring complex fallback logic or acceptance of energy_per_ton measurement uncertainty beyond acceptable limits.
Reporting cadence lock may conflict with operational needs for multiple aggregation windows (e.g., per-cycle for diagnostics, per-shift for compliance), requiring trade-off analysis.

Energy calculation algorithm diffusion risk persists if NGHE Solution architecture does not enforce module boundaries; external systems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) may independently implement similar calculations, creating inconsistency.
Sensor calibration certification process may not be formalized, leading to calibration drift over time and energy_per_ton measurement degradation.
evidence_needed: Sensor interface specification document: signal format, sampling rate, resolution, calibration procedure, hardware integration points, and responsibility split between Vehicle and NGHE Solution. Acceptance: interface specification reviewed and approved by both Vehicle and NGHE Solution teams.

Network link reliability SLA: maximum acceptable latency, packet loss tolerance, buffering strategy, failover/retry logic, and uptime requirement (e.g., ≥99.5%). Acceptance: SLA validated against existing network infrastructure or new infrastructure requirements identified.

Tasking input data contract: required operational context fields, data freshness requirements, validation rules, and fallback behavior. Acceptance: contract reviewed by Tasking system owner and NGHE Solution team; fallback behavior tested.

Reporting cadence specification: periodic reporting interval (e.g., per-shift, per-mission) and aggregation window for energy_per_ton calculation. Acceptance: reporting cadence locked and traced to acceptance criteria evaluation period.

Energy calculation algorithm specification: formula, normalization factors, outlier handling, rounding rules, calibration adjustments, and module ownership. Acceptance: algorithm specification reviewed and centralized in single NGHE Solution code artifact.

Sensor calibration certification procedure: calibration accuracy requirement (e.g., ±2%), certification process, recalibration interval, and measurement uncertainty budget allocation. Acceptance: calibration procedure formalized and first sensor batch certified before deployment.

Acceptance criteria traceability matrix: map each containment risk to specific acceptance criterion or test case (e.g., 'sensor calibration accuracy ±2%' → energy_per_ton measurement uncertainty budget; 'network uptime ≥99.5%' → data completeness for evaluation period). Acceptance: traceability matrix reviewed and test cases defined.
